content.assessment: Statutory consolidation with mitigation directly confronts the highest-intensity political flashpoint identified in the evidence: closure decisions have repeatedly triggered mobilization capable of reversing central decisions, and a well-organized national education coalition (unions, parents, students) has already demonstrated the capacity to raise the political cost of reorganization. Formalizing closure procedure may reduce arbitrary reversal but does not eliminate the underlying opposition intensity, and durability will depend on whether mitigation guarantees are perceived as credible rather than cosmetic.
opportunities: Severe, well-documented depopulation cliffs (147 localities facing eventual full depopulation) create objective, defensible criteria that could reduce perceived arbitrariness and thus opposition intensity if closure is clearly tied to demographic fact rather than discretionary choice (F-live-political_feasibility-05)
strengths: Addresses a documented reversal mechanism directly: formal procedure and consultation rights could reduce (though not eliminate) the kind of ad hoc protest-driven reversal already observed (F-live-political_feasibility-07)
Managed, codified closure with guarantees may be more legitimate and durable than the current administratively opaque process, potentially reducing distrust that fuels large-scale mobilization
threats: Historical precedent shows closure-oriented policies can provoke sufficient backlash to force reversal even after formal announcement, suggesting statutory guarantees may not be sufficient to prevent political destabilization if mitigation is seen as inadequate (F-live-political_feasibility-07)

Long-run historical record of policy reversals tied to changes in political regime suggests any statutory consolidation framework enacted by the current coalition faces meaningful risk of being undone or reworked by a future government (F-live-political_feasibility-09)
weaknesses: A pre-existing, large-scale organized coalition (unions, parents, students; 50,000+ demonstrators in 2022) opposing education policy generally raises the baseline political cost of any consolidation-based reform, statutory or not (F-live-political_feasibility-08)

Centralized authority structure (KLIK) already generates budget dysfunction that could undermine credibility of mitigation funding commitments (transport, compensation) promised under this proposal (F-live-political_feasibility-06)
